content: Earth’s seasons arise primarily from its axial tilt of approximately 23.5° relative to the orbital plane, which modulates the solar declination and thus the latitudinal distribution of insolation throughout the year. This obliquity causes the subsolar point to migrate between the Tropics of Cancer and Capricorn, altering both day length and solar zenith angle, which in turn drive the cyclical variation in surface energy input that characterizes seasonal changes. While Earth’s orbital eccentricity (~0.0167) introduces slight asymmetries in seasonal duration and intensity—perihelion occurring near the boreal winter—this effect is subordinate to axial tilt in controlling seasonal amplitude. Over longer timescales, Milankovitch cycles modulate obliquity, precession, and eccentricity, inducing variations in seasonal insolation patterns that underpin paleoclimatic shifts. Consequently, the interplay of Earth’s obliquity-driven insolation gradients and orbital parameters governs not only the timing and intensity of seasons but also their variability within the Quaternary climatic framework.
